content.text: is a software testing approach where multiple application components, modules, or services are joined and tested together to evaluate how well they work when combined. The focus is on testing the
between integrated parts, rather than testing components in isolation.
independently developed units of software work correctly when they are connected to each other
. They test how modules interact through APIs, databases, file systems, and network connections.

Without integration testing:
- Components work alone but fail when combined
- API calls break due to mismatched data formats
- Database queries return incorrect results in production
- Service-to-service communication fails silently
- Bugs only surface after deployment, causing production failures
that unit tests miss, ensuring the system works as a cohesive whole.
Ensures data passes correctly between modules
Uses actual databases, APIs, file systems
Validates modules work together as expected
Catches issues that unit tests miss
Takes seconds to minutes per test (vs milliseconds for unit)
Requires real databases, services, test environments
One failing dependency breaks multiple tests
Doesn't test full system flows (need system tests)
Your Module → Real Database/API → Actual Response
Unlike unit tests that mock everything, integration tests use
According to Martin Fowler, there are
Exercise only the portion of code that talks to a separate service
(mocks/stubs) of those services, either in-process or remote
Scope similar to unit tests, run with same test framework
Fast, providing quick feedback in deployment pipeline

# Narrow: Test shopping cart code that talks to catalog
# Uses mock catalog service
def test_cart_calculates_with_catalog_prices():
    mock_catalog = MockCatalog()
    mock_catalog.get_price.return_value = 10.0
    
    cart = ShoppingCart(mock_catalog)
    cart.add_item("widget")
    
    assert cart.total() == 10.0
Need substantial test environment and network access
Slower, run later in deployment pipeline

# Broad: Test entire cart + catalog system together
# Uses real catalog service
def test_cart_with_real_catalog():
    catalog_service = start_real_catalog_service()
    cart = ShoppingCart(catalog_service)
    
    cart.add_item("widget")
    
    assert cart.total() == catalog_service.get_price("widget")
: Initialize real dependencies (database connection, API server)
: Assert that data flows correctly and final state is correct
✅ Yes - verify SQL works with real DB
✅ Yes - test file I/O with real files
: Don't use integration tests for testing pure business logic or isolated functions—those need unit tests.

import pytest
from sqlalchemy import create_engine
from myapp.models import User, engine

@pytest.fixture
def test_db():
    # Create test database
    test_engine = create_engine('sqlite:///test.db')
    
    # Create tables
    User.metadata.create_all(test_engine)
    
    yield test_engine
    
    # Cleanup
    test_engine.dispose()

def test_create_and_retrieve_user(test_db):
    # Create user in real database
    user = User(name="Alice", email="alice@example.com")
    session = test_db.connect()
    session.add(user)
    session.commit()
    
    # Retrieve from database
    retrieved = session.query(User).filter_by(name="Alice").first()
    
    # Assert both operations work together
    assert retrieved.email == "alice@example.com"
    assert retrieved.id is not None
Example: Integration Test with Mock External Service (Narrow)

from unittest.mock import Mock

def test_payment_processing_with_mocked_gateway():
    # Create mock payment gateway (test double)
    mock_gateway = Mock()
    mock_gateway.process.return_value = {"status": "success", "transaction_id": "txn_123"}
    
    # Use mock in payment service
    payment_service = PaymentService(mock_gateway)
    result = payment_service.charge(user_id=1, amount=50.00)
    
    # Verify interaction
    assert result.success == True
    mock_gateway.process.assert_called_once_with(amount=50.00)
Integration tests that alter databases need careful state management:
Cleanup after each test (delete test data)
Ensure cleanup runs even on failure
Wrap test in transaction, rollback at end
Snapshot before test, roll back after
for atomicity—write, read, and delete in one transaction that rolls back.
Test all modules together at once

┌─────────────────┐
           │  System Tests   │  ← Few (1–10), slow (minutes)
           │ (End-to-End)    │     Full application flows
           ├─────────────────┤
           │ Integration     │  ← Medium (10–100), moderate (seconds)
           │   Tests         │     APIs, databases, services [web:36]
           ├─────────────────┤
           │ Unit Tests      │  ← Many (100–1000+), fast (ms)
           │                 │     Individual functions [web:25]
           └─────────────────┘

: 500+ tests covering: - 200+ API endpoint tests - 150+ database query tests - 100+ service-to-service tests - 50+ external API tests

: Unit tests on every commit (30s), integration tests on nightly builds (5–10 min)
-
: 95%+ bug-free releases, minimal integration bugs in production
Using mocks instead of real dependencies
- test one integration point per test
- run integration tests on every merge
- mostly narrow, some broad for critical paths
: Combine unit + integration tests for comprehensive coverage. Use narrow integration tests for speed, broad for critical paths.
